file_name: T - 2023 - Skin cancer cell detection using optimization algorithms.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) to optimize the parameters (number of hidden layers and neurons) of a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) neural network for the classification of skin cancer cells (malignant melanoma and nevus). The study reports an accuracy of 85.6% using this AI-driven approach.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a 'Proposed Work' section detailing the use of Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) to optimize a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) for skin cancer cell detection. It includes specific experimental results, such as an accuracy of 85.6% on a dataset of 170 samples, and compares these results against existing work, indicating it is an original experimental study rather than a review.

title: Skin Cancer Cell Detection Using Optimization Algorithms
journal: International Journal of Advanced Trends in Engineering and Management (IJATEM)
brief_summary: This study addresses the detection of skin cancer cells, specifically distinguishing between malignant melanoma and nevus, using an optimized machine learning approach. The authors employed Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) to determine the optimal architecture, including the number of hidden layers and neurons, for a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) neural network. The experimental dataset consisted of 170 samples, with 1875 extracted features per sample, split into training, validation, and testing sets using 3-fold cross-validation. The model's performance was evaluated against existing work by Giotis et al. using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics. The proposed PSO-optimized MLP achieved an accuracy of 85.6%, with 84% sensitivity and 83% specificity, outperforming the comparative baseline. The results indicate that the system achieves higher accuracy than the typical clinical accuracy of dermatologists for melanoma diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection in the proposed experiment. The dataset contained 170 samples including malignant melanoma and nevus. The paper notes it is the riskiest skin cancer with low endurance rates.
The comparator class in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 170 samples including malignant melanoma and nevus. The paper discusses distinguishing melanoma from benign moles.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin cancer cells (malignant melanoma vs. nevus)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'classification of the dataset' using a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) to distinguish between 'malignant melanoma and nevus'. Performance is evaluated using accuracy (85.6%), sensitivity (84%), and specificity (83%).

datasets_used.name: Custom Dataset (Malignant Melanoma and Nevus)
datasets_used.description: A private dataset consisting of 170 skin cancer cell images containing malignant melanoma and nevus classes. The specific source or collection method is not detailed in the text, but it was used for the proposed PSO-optimized MLP experiment.

use.training: 119 images (70%) used for training the Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) optimized by Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to split the dataset into training, validation, and testing sets for model evaluation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests were reported in the paper. The study compares performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) of the proposed PSO-optimized MLP model against existing work (Giotis et al.) and clinical accuracy, but does not perform statistical significance testing.

Custom Dataset (Malignant Melanoma and N.notes: The proposed PSO-optimized MLP achieved an accuracy of 85.6%, which is 2.6% higher than the baseline Giotis et al. [6]. The system also achieved 2.9% better accuracy than the reported clinical accuracy of dermatologists (85%) for melanoma diagnosis. The dataset consisted of 170 samples (119 training, 26 validation, 25 testing) with 1875 features per sample.

architecture_details.neurons_per_layer: Optimized via Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), with a maximum of 500 neurons per layer
architecture_details.feature_extraction: 1875 features extracted from segmented images (25 features excluded as constant)

hyperparameters.acceleration_coefficients: c1 (individual particle learning rate), c2 (social influence)
hyperparameters.random_variables: r1, r2 (uniformly distributed random numbers between 0 and 1)

training_details.optimizer_details: PSO used to optimize the number of hidden layers and neurons

preprocessing_techniques: The paper mentions that the general process for skin cancer detection includes preprocessing steps such as hair removal, de-noising, sharpening, and resizing of the skin image (Abstract).

However, for the specific proposed work using PSO-optimized MLP, the paper does not report applying these preprocessing techniques to their custom dataset of 170 samples; it states that features were extracted directly from segmented images and 25 constant features were excluded.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used in the related work [1] (Kopec et al.) to deal with class imbalance problems. Not used in the proposed PSO-optimized MLP work.

conclusions: The proposed PSO-optimized Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) achieved an accuracy of 85.6%, sensitivity of 84%, and specificity of 83%, outperforming the baseline work by Giotis et al. (83% accuracy, 82% sensitivity, 80% specificity).
The system achieved 2.9% better accuracy than the reported clinical accuracy of dermatologists (85%) for the diagnosis of malignant melanoma.
Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) allows for faster searching of optimized neural network parameters compared to exhaustive search methods.
The study utilized all 1875 extracted features for the classification task without performing feature selection.
The authors acknowledge that feature selection and transformation are necessary future work to reduce feature dimensionality.
important_remarks: The study uses a very small, private dataset of only 170 samples (119 training, 26 validation, 25 testing), which limits statistical power and generalizability.
The source and collection method of the custom dataset are not detailed, raising concerns about potential selection bias and reproducibility.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the internal test set.
The paper does not report statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance differences between the proposed method and the baseline (Giotis et al.).

The claim that the system achieves '2.9% better accuracy than the clinical accuracy by dermatologists' relies on a cited clinical accuracy of 85% (Ref [19]) without providing direct comparative clinical data or context on the dermatologists' expertise level.

All 1875 extracted features were used without feature selection, despite the authors acknowledging that feature selection is necessary to reduce dimensionality, potentially leading to overfitting or noise inclusion.

The baseline comparison is against Giotis et al. [6], which used non-dermoscopic images, whereas the current study does not explicitly specify the image modality (dermoscopic vs. non-dermoscopic) for its own dataset, making the comparison potentially unfair or unclear.
The paper does not address class imbalance in the dataset, although it mentions SMOTE in the context of other related works.
